title: Brain Tumours — Primary & Metastatic
oneLiner: Space-occupying lesions from glial cells (primary) or systemic cancers (metastatic) causing focal deficits, seizures, and raised intracranial pressure — common, dangerous, and often missed.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic Vignette:**  
A 55-year-old female with no past medical history presents to ED with a 3-week history of worsening morning headaches, nausea without vomiting, and new-onset left arm weakness. She says her husband noticed she sounds “slurred” in the evenings. On examination, she has mild left-sided pronator drift, papilloedema on fundoscopy, and a subtle left homonymous hemianopia. She is alert but slow to respond.  

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- **Morning headaches** → Worse with lying supine overnight → CSF stasis, raised ICP → Classic for intracranial mass
- **Nausea without vomiting** → Stimulation of area postrema (chemoreceptor trigger zone) by increased ICP or direct brainstem compression
- **New-onset focal weakness (left arm)** → Contralateral motor cortex (right frontal/parietal) involvement – either tumor itself or surrounding oedema
- **Slurred speech** → Expressive dysphasia (if Broca’s area) or motor coordination (cerebellar) – can be subtle
- **Pronator drift (left)** → Upper motor neuron sign contralateral to lesion – corticospinal tract
- **Papilloedema** → Increased ICP transmitted via optic nerve sheath – sign of chronic raised pressure
- **Homonymous hemianopia** → Optic radiation (parietal or temporal lobe) – indicates mass effect on posterior visual pathways
- **Alert but slow** → Early frontal lobe syndrome (personality change, abulia) or diffuse cerebral oedema

**Atypical presentations:**
- **Stroke mimic:** Sudden onset hemiparesis due to intratumoural haemorrhage (e.g., glioblastoma, melanoma metastasis) – but neuroimaging shows mass with blood product
- **Psychiatric onset:** Isolated apathy, disinhibition, or depression – often assumed mood disorder until imaging done for decline or falls
- **Seizure-only presentation:** Young adult with first tonic-clonic seizure – workup reveals low-grade glioma (e.g., oligodendroglioma) or small cortical metastasis
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**  
Brain tumours are abnormal growths within the skull – either arising from brain tissue itself (primary) or spread from another part of the body (metastatic). They occupy space, compress normal structures, disrupt blood flow and CSF circulation, and can cause seizures, paralysis, and death if untreated.

**Why this matters clinically:**  
- **Frequency:** Metastases outnumber primaries 10:1 – any patient with cancer, especially lung, breast, melanoma, or colorectal, can present with brain involvement. Primary brain tumours are less common but highly lethal (e.g., glioblastoma has a median survival of ~15 months).
- **Stakes:** Delay in diagnosis can lead to irreversible neurological damage, herniation, and death. First presentation may be to A&E or GP – **you must not miss** a mass lesion in a patient with new headache, seizure, or focal sign.
- **Common pitfalls:** Attributing headache to migraine or tension; ignoring subtle cognitive change in the elderly; omitting fundoscopy in headache patients; failing to start dexamethasone early for vasogenic oedema.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story of development:**  
A brain tumour begins as a clone of cells that proliferate uncontrollably. Primary tumours often arise from glial support cells (astrocytes → astrocytoma/glioblastoma; oligodendrocytes → oligodendroglioma) or from meninges (meningioma), pituitary, etc. Metastatic tumours embolise from a distant primary – haematogenous spread via arterial circulation (common: lung, breast) – and lodge at grey-white matter junction where blood flow slows.

As the tumour grows, **mass effect** displaces brain structures. **Vasogenic oedema** forms because tumour blood-brain barrier is leaky (leaky capillaries) – fluid accumulates in extracellular space, more in white matter than grey. This oedema is why dexamethasone works (steroids tighten the BBB). The tumour also disrupts CSF absorption (increased ICP), compresses ventricles (hydrocephalus if midline shift or aqueduct block), and may cause focal irritation (seizures from peritumoural cortex).

**Mechanism → Sign:**
- **Papilloedema** → Increased ICP transmitted to optic nerve sheath → axoplasmic flow stasis → optic disc swelling
- **Morning headache** → ICP highest after lying flat overnight (CSF pressure rises) → head pain from meningeal stretch and trigeminovascular activation
- **Focal deficit** → Direct compression or oedema of motor/sensory cortex or tracts → e.g., leg weakness = parasagittal tumour, arm/face = lateral frontal
- **Seizure** → Irritative focus from tumour cells + oedema + local ischaemia → especially low-grade gliomas (slow-growing, epileptogenic) or cortical metastases
- **Personality change** → Frontal lobe (disinhibition, apathy, abulia) or temporal lobe (aggression, hallucinations)

**Correlation to pattern recognition:**  
The vignette’s morning headache, papilloedema, and focal weakness are all downstream of raised ICP and oedema. The homonymous hemianopia places the lesion posteriorly (occipital or temporal). Because she has both motor and visual symptoms, the lesion is likely large or multifocal (e.g., glioblastoma or multiple metastases).
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Cause | Stem Clue (how you might spot it) |
|----------|-------|-----------------------------------|
| **Primary – Glial** | Astrocytoma (low-grade) | Young adult with first seizure; long history of headaches; slow progression |
| | Glioblastoma (WHO grade IV) | >60, rapid decline, ring-enhancing lesion with central necrosis on MRI |
| | Oligodendroglioma | Seizures; often frontal; calcified on CT; 1p/19q codeletion favourable |
| **Primary – Non-glial** | Meningioma | Female, incidental, extra-axial lesion with dural tail on MRI – often benign |
| | Pituitary adenoma | Visual field defect (bitemporal hemianopia); endocrine symptoms (galactorrhoea, acromegaly) |
| | Medulloblastoma | Posterior fossa mass in child (<10), truncal ataxia, hydrocephalus |
| **Metastatic** | Lung (most common) | Smoker, weight loss, haemoptysis – isolated or multiple cortical/subcortical lesions |
| | Breast | History of breast cancer; hormone receptor +; often multiple |
| | Melanoma | History of skin lesion; highly haemorrhagic metastases; jet-black on MRI |
| | Colorectal, Renal, Others | Usually advanced stage; check primary staging |
| **Idiopathic** | Most primary brain tumours | No clear risk factor; only extreme radiation exposure linked to meningioma/glioma |

**Most common cause overall:** Lung cancer metastasis (especially in those ≥50 with smoking history). In non-cancer patients with a single brain lesion, glioblastoma is the most likely primary.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:**  
No formal scoring system – diagnosis is imaging plus histology. But the **Response Assessment in Neuro-Oncology (RANO)** criteria exist for follow-up (measuring enhancing tumour on MRI).

**First-line test (urgent) – CT head (non-contrast):**
- **What it shows:** Mass lesion, oedema (hypodensity), midline shift, hydrocephalus, haemorrhage (hyperdense). May see calcification (oligodendroglioma, meningioma).
- **Why:** Rapid rule-out of haemorrhage or large mass causing herniation – emergency first step.
- **Interpretation:** A ring-enhancing lesion with surrounding oedema is **highly suspicious** for glioblastoma or abscess. Multiple ring-enhancing lesions = metastases until proven otherwise.

**Gold-standard – MRI brain with gadolinium:**
- **What it shows:** T1 pre- and post-contrast (enhancement = BBB breakdown), T2/FLAIR (oedema), diffusion-weighted (abscess vs tumour), MR spectroscopy (choline peak in tumour).
- **Why:** Better resolution, can differentiate tumour from oedema, assess for leptomeningeal spread.
- **Key patterns:**  
  - **Glioblastoma:** Irregular ring enhancement, central necrosis, “butterfly” across corpus callosum  
  - **Meningioma:** Extra-axial, dural tail, homogenous enhancement, hyperostosis  
  - **Metastasis:** Multiple, small round, at grey-white junction, uniform ring enhancement  
  - **Abscess:** Diffusion restriction, smooth wall, ring enhancement – **key mimic**

**Histology (via biopsy or resection):**
- **What it shows:** Tumour type, grade, molecular markers (IDH1 mutation, MGMT methylation, 1p/19q codeletion).
- **Why:** Prognosis and treatment decisions (e.g., IDH-mutant gliomas better prognosis; MGMT-methylated glioblastoma more chemo-sensitive).

**Single finding that confirms:**  
- **Biopsy** – a definite diagnosis of malignancy.  
- **Imaging** – multiple ring-enhancing lesions at grey-white junction in a patient with known cancer = almost certainly metastatic.

**Rule-out:**  
- **Normal MRI with gadolinium** (high-sensitivity) makes brain tumour very unlikely – consider seizure workup, metabolic, or functional disorders.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) – the “raise ICP” protocol:**
- **Call senior / neurosurgery** – any new mass with midline shift >5mm or impending herniation requires immediate neurosurgical opinion.
- **Elevate head of bed to 30°** – improves venous drainage, reduces ICP.
- **Dexamethasone IV 10 mg stat** – then 4-6 mg QDS (oral/IV) – reduces vasogenic oedema (tightens BBB). Delay if isolated mass – only give after imaging confirms no haemorrhage or infection risk (abscess can worsen with steroids).
- **Mannitol 20% 1-2 g/kg IV** if signs of herniation (Cushing’s triad: bradycardia, hypertension, irregular breathing) – temporary measure until theatre.
- **Antiepileptic if seizure** – Levetiracetam 1000 mg IV (loading) – preferred due to low drug interactions (enzyme-inducing AEDs like phenytoin can interfere with steroids/chemo).
- **Do NOT** give prophylactic anticonvulsants for newly discovered tumour without seizure – evidence shows no benefit and side effects.

**Ward (24-72h) – stabilisation and planning:**
- **Continue dexamethasone** – taper as symptoms improve (e.g., reduce by 2 mg every 3-5 days over weeks). Monitor blood glucose, GI prophylaxis (PPI).
- **CT scan or MRI with CT angiogram** if surgical plan – evaluate vascular anatomy.
- **Neurosurgery consult** – decide on biopsy vs maximal safe resection. For a single accessible lesion, resection is preferred for both diagnosis and debulking.
- **If multiple metastases** – whole brain radiotherapy (WBRT) or stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) may be planned. Start dexamethasone before WBRT.
- **Check for primary** if metastatic suspected – CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, PET-CT, tumour markers.

**Long-term – definitive treatment (example regimens):**
- **Glioblastoma (standard)** : Maximal safe resection → concurrent radiotherapy (60 Gy in 30 fractions) + **Temozolomide** 75 mg/m² PO daily during RT → then 6 cycles of adjuvant Temozolomide 150-200 mg/m² PO days 1-5 every 28 days. MGMT methylation predicts response.
- **Low-grade glioma** : Observation vs surgery alone, then radiation +/- chemo (PCV or Temozolomide) for high-risk features (age >40, subtotal resection, IDH wild-type).
- **Meningioma** : If incidental + small → watch. If symptomatic or growing → surgical resection (Simpson grade I). Radiotherapy for subtotal or high-grade/atypical.
- **Metastases (solitary)** : Resection + SRS for single; WBRT if multiple or leptomeningeal. For radiosensitive histologies (e.g., SCLC, germ cell) – consider WBRT first.

**What NOT to do:**
- **Do not start dexamethasone before imaging** if infection possible (abscess) – steroids can mask fever and reduce enhancement, delaying culture.
- **Do not give whole brain RT for a single metastasis if good performance status** – better to resect + SRS to preserve cognition.
- **Do not use prophylactic anticonvulsants** – unnecessary and causes side effects.
- **Do not taper steroids too fast** – rebound oedema, check symptoms.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Brain Tumour (Primary or Metastatic) | Brain Abscess | Cerebral Ischaemia (Stroke) | Multiple Sclerosis (MS) |
|---------|--------------------------------------|--------------|----------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Pain** | Headache (morning, worse with Valsalva, progressive) | Headache, fever, signs of infection | Usually no headache (unless haemorrhagic) | Headache not typical (can have optic neuritis pain) |
| **Exam** | Focal deficit, papilloedema, seizures, cognitive change | May have same, plus meningism (neck stiffness, Kernig’s) | Acute onset of maximal deficit within minutes-hours (stroke) | Relapsing-remitting episodes; optic neuritis, Lhermitte’s sign, Uthhoff phenomenon |
| **Imaging** | MRI: mass, enhancement, oedema. Multiple lesions (met) | Ring-enhancing lesion with central diffusion restriction; smooth wall | DWI restriction (acute ischaemia), no mass effect (unless large) | Periventricular, juxtacortical, Dawsons fingers, enhancing lesions (active plaques) |
| **Labs** | Normal or mild CRP (if no infection). Tumour markers maybe | Raised CRP/PCT, positive blood culture, procalcitonin high | Normal; lipid profile for risk | CSF: oligoclonal bands, elevated IgG index, mild pleocytosis |
| **Key differ** | Histology (biopsy) is definitive | Clinical + diffusion-weighted MRI restriction | Rapid onset + vascular territory; CT/CTA shows occlusion | Age <50, relapsing history, CSF findings, no mass effect |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Major complication 1 – Transtentorial herniation (uncal):**
- **Presentation:** Rapid decline in GCS, ipsilateral fixed/dilated pupil (CN III compression), contralateral hemiparesis (or ipsilateral if Kernohan’s notch), Cushing’s triad, posturing.
- **What to do:** Immediate mannitol, hyperventilation (PaCO2 30-35 mmHg), raise head, call neurosurgery – emergency craniectomy/evacuation.
- **Pattern:** Late exam stem – missed slow-growing tumour then sudden collapse after head CT shows severe midline shift.

**Major complication 2 – Seizure status epilepticus:**
- **Presentation:** Continuous convulsions or non-convulsive status (altered mental state but no tonic-clonic movements) – needs EEG if suspicious.
- **What to do:** ABC, midazolam 10 mg buccal/IM, then levetiracetam 60 mg/kg IV, if refractory, propofol infusion + ITU.
- **Key sign:** New “todd’s paresis” (post-ictal weakness) lasting >24h may indicate underlying tumour.

**Major complication 3 – Hydrocephalus:**
- **Presentation:** Worsening headache, vomiting, decreasing GCS, upgaze palsy (Parinaud), “setting sun” sign in children.
- **Imaging:** Ventriculomegaly with periventricular ooze on MRI.
- **Management:** External ventricular drain (EVD) or VP shunt – urgent neurosurgery.

**Late presentation patterns:**  
A patient with untreated low-grade glioma may present years later with catastrophic bleed, or with secondary glioblastoma after dedifferentiation. Untreated metastasis can cause drop attacks, spinal seeding, or leptomeningeal carcinomatosis.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic for “Brain tumour red flags on history” – “SUNBURN”:**
- **S** – Seizure (new onset in adult)
- **U** – Unexplained vomiting (morning)
- **N** – New focal neurological deficit (motor/sensory/visual)
- **B** – Behavioural change (frontal lobe)
- **U** – Unremitting headache (progressive, worse with coughing/lying)
- **R** – Recent cancer diagnosis (especially lung, breast, melanoma)
- **N** – Nausea + headache without fever

**Mnemonic for “Causes of ring-enhancing brain lesions” – “MAGIC DR”:**
- **M** – Metastasis
- **A** – Abscess
- **G** – Glioblastoma
- **I** – Infarct (subacute)
- **C** – Contusion
- **D** – Demyelinating disease (tumefactive MS)
- **R** – Radiation necrosis

**High-yield clinical pearls:**
1. **Morning headache + papilloedema = raised ICP until proven otherwise.** Don’t waste time with migraine diagnosis – get imaging.
2. **New-onset seizure in an adult is a brain tumour until proven otherwise.** (Except in known epilepsy or clear provoked cause). The most common cause is metastatic lung cancer.
3. **Multiple ring-enhancing lesions = metastatic disease > abscess > lymphoma.** In an immunocompromised patient, consider toxoplasmosis (multiple ring-enhancing, but usually basal ganglia).
4. **Dexamethasone does NOT treat the tumour** – it only reduces oedema. It can mask progression – always document GCS and neurology before starting.
5. **A “butterfly” across the corpus callosum is classic for glioblastoma** – it spreads along white matter tracts and is unresectable.
6. **Meningiomas are often incidental and found in women over 50.** They are benign but can cause seizures or mass effect if large – watch and scan annually.
7. **In a patient with known cancer and new headache + focal sign, do immediate MRI brain with contrast.** CT can miss small metastases and posterior fossa lesions.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- Missing fundoscopy in headache patients.
- Starting antiepileptic drugs without a clear seizure diagnosis (can delay tumour workup).
- Assuming single brain lesion is always a primary – must rule out metastasis with CT chest/abdomen.
- Forgetting to think of brain abscess in febrile patients with ring lesion (especially if dental infection, sinusitis, or IV drug use).
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior (reg/consult) urgently:**
- GCS drop ≥2 points from baseline
- New focal deficit or worsening papilloedema
- Seizure (first, prolonged, or recurrent)
- Suspected herniation (pupil asymmetry, Cushing’s, posturing)
- New hydrocephalus on imaging (ventricular dilation)

**When to call ICU / crash team:**
- GCS ≤8 (protection of airway)
- Status epilepticus (persistent >5 min or recurrence without recovery)
- Signs of herniation requiring immediate ICP management (mannitol, hyperventilation, consider intubation)
- Refractory raised ICP not responding to medical therapy

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS2 ≥7** or single parameter of concern (e.g., RR <8 or >25, SpO2 <91%, systolic BP <90 or >220)
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** (if septic or haemorrhagic)
- **GCS <9** (consider intubation)
- **Midline shift >10mm** on CT (high risk of herniation – discuss with neurosurgery emergently)
- **CT showing intraventricular haemorrhage or posterior fossa mass** – these can cause rapid deterioration due to hydrocephalus or brainstem compression

---

**Final clinical take-home:**  
When a patient with a new neurological symptom walks in, **think brain tumour first**, do a proper neurological exam including fundoscopy, and get imaging early. Dexamethasone is your friend, but only after you have the scan. Don’t be the doctor who says “it’s just a migraine” and misses a glioblastoma.

rawMarkdown:
## Brain Tumours — Primary & Metastatic

**One-liner:** Space-occupying lesions from glial cells (primary) or systemic cancers (metastatic) causing focal deficits, seizures, and raised intracranial pressure — common, dangerous, and often missed.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic Vignette:**  
A 55-year-old female with no past medical history presents to ED with a 3-week history of worsening morning headaches, nausea without vomiting, and new-onset left arm weakness. She says her husband noticed she sounds “slurred” in the evenings. On examination, she has mild left-sided pronator drift, papilloedema on fundoscopy, and a subtle left homonymous hemianopia. She is alert but slow to respond.  

**Dissecting the vignette:**
- **Morning headaches** → Worse with lying supine overnight → CSF stasis, raised ICP → Classic for intracranial mass
- **Nausea without vomiting** → Stimulation of area postrema (chemoreceptor trigger zone) by increased ICP or direct brainstem compression
- **New-onset focal weakness (left arm)** → Contralateral motor cortex (right frontal/parietal) involvement – either tumor itself or surrounding oedema
- **Slurred speech** → Expressive dysphasia (if Broca’s area) or motor coordination (cerebellar) – can be subtle
- **Pronator drift (left)** → Upper motor neuron sign contralateral to lesion – corticospinal tract
- **Papilloedema** → Increased ICP transmitted via optic nerve sheath – sign of chronic raised pressure
- **Homonymous hemianopia** → Optic radiation (parietal or temporal lobe) – indicates mass effect on posterior visual pathways
- **Alert but slow** → Early frontal lobe syndrome (personality change, abulia) or diffuse cerebral oedema

**Atypical presentations:**
- **Stroke mimic:** Sudden onset hemiparesis due to intratumoural haemorrhage (e.g., glioblastoma, melanoma metastasis) – but neuroimaging shows mass with blood product
- **Psychiatric onset:** Isolated apathy, disinhibition, or depression – often assumed mood disorder until imaging done for decline or falls
- **Seizure-only presentation:** Young adult with first tonic-clonic seizure – workup reveals low-grade glioma (e.g., oligodendroglioma) or small cortical metastasis

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**  
Brain tumours are abnormal growths within the skull – either arising from brain tissue itself (primary) or spread from another part of the body (metastatic). They occupy space, compress normal structures, disrupt blood flow and CSF circulation, and can cause seizures, paralysis, and death if untreated.

**Why this matters clinically:**  
- **Frequency:** Metastases outnumber primaries 10:1 – any patient with cancer, especially lung, breast, melanoma, or colorectal, can present with brain involvement. Primary brain tumours are less common but highly lethal (e.g., glioblastoma has a median survival of ~15 months).
- **Stakes:** Delay in diagnosis can lead to irreversible neurological damage, herniation, and death. First presentation may be to A&E or GP – **you must not miss** a mass lesion in a patient with new headache, seizure, or focal sign.
- **Common pitfalls:** Attributing headache to migraine or tension; ignoring subtle cognitive change in the elderly; omitting fundoscopy in headache patients; failing to start dexamethasone early for vasogenic oedema.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story of development:**  
A brain tumour begins as a clone of cells that proliferate uncontrollably. Primary tumours often arise from glial support cells (astrocytes → astrocytoma/glioblastoma; oligodendrocytes → oligodendroglioma) or from meninges (meningioma), pituitary, etc. Metastatic tumours embolise from a distant primary – haematogenous spread via arterial circulation (common: lung, breast) – and lodge at grey-white matter junction where blood flow slows.

As the tumour grows, **mass effect** displaces brain structures. **Vasogenic oedema** forms because tumour blood-brain barrier is leaky (leaky capillaries) – fluid accumulates in extracellular space, more in white matter than grey. This oedema is why dexamethasone works (steroids tighten the BBB). The tumour also disrupts CSF absorption (increased ICP), compresses ventricles (hydrocephalus if midline shift or aqueduct block), and may cause focal irritation (seizures from peritumoural cortex).

**Mechanism → Sign:**
- **Papilloedema** → Increased ICP transmitted to optic nerve sheath → axoplasmic flow stasis → optic disc swelling
- **Morning headache** → ICP highest after lying flat overnight (CSF pressure rises) → head pain from meningeal stretch and trigeminovascular activation
- **Focal deficit** → Direct compression or oedema of motor/sensory cortex or tracts → e.g., leg weakness = parasagittal tumour, arm/face = lateral frontal
- **Seizure** → Irritative focus from tumour cells + oedema + local ischaemia → especially low-grade gliomas (slow-growing, epileptogenic) or cortical metastases
- **Personality change** → Frontal lobe (disinhibition, apathy, abulia) or temporal lobe (aggression, hallucinations)

**Correlation to pattern recognition:**  
The vignette’s morning headache, papilloedema, and focal weakness are all downstream of raised ICP and oedema. The homonymous hemianopia places the lesion posteriorly (occipital or temporal). Because she has both motor and visual symptoms, the lesion is likely large or multifocal (e.g., glioblastoma or multiple metastases).

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Cause | Stem Clue (how you might spot it) |
|----------|-------|-----------------------------------|
| **Primary – Glial** | Astrocytoma (low-grade) | Young adult with first seizure; long history of headaches; slow progression |
| | Glioblastoma (WHO grade IV) | >60, rapid decline, ring-enhancing lesion with central necrosis on MRI |
| | Oligodendroglioma | Seizures; often frontal; calcified on CT; 1p/19q codeletion favourable |
| **Primary – Non-glial** | Meningioma | Female, incidental, extra-axial lesion with dural tail on MRI – often benign |
| | Pituitary adenoma | Visual field defect (bitemporal hemianopia); endocrine symptoms (galactorrhoea, acromegaly) |
| | Medulloblastoma | Posterior fossa mass in child (<10), truncal ataxia, hydrocephalus |
| **Metastatic** | Lung (most common) | Smoker, weight loss, haemoptysis – isolated or multiple cortical/subcortical lesions |
| | Breast | History of breast cancer; hormone receptor +; often multiple |
| | Melanoma | History of skin lesion; highly haemorrhagic metastases; jet-black on MRI |
| | Colorectal, Renal, Others | Usually advanced stage; check primary staging |
| **Idiopathic** | Most primary brain tumours | No clear risk factor; only extreme radiation exposure linked to meningioma/glioma |

**Most common cause overall:** Lung cancer metastasis (especially in those ≥50 with smoking history). In non-cancer patients with a single brain lesion, glioblastoma is the most likely primary.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:**  
No formal scoring system – diagnosis is imaging plus histology. But the **Response Assessment in Neuro-Oncology (RANO)** criteria exist for follow-up (measuring enhancing tumour on MRI).

**First-line test (urgent) – CT head (non-contrast):**
- **What it shows:** Mass lesion, oedema (hypodensity), midline shift, hydrocephalus, haemorrhage (hyperdense). May see calcification (oligodendroglioma, meningioma).
- **Why:** Rapid rule-out of haemorrhage or large mass causing herniation – emergency first step.
- **Interpretation:** A ring-enhancing lesion with surrounding oedema is **highly suspicious** for glioblastoma or abscess. Multiple ring-enhancing lesions = metastases until proven otherwise.

**Gold-standard – MRI brain with gadolinium:**
- **What it shows:** T1 pre- and post-contrast (enhancement = BBB breakdown), T2/FLAIR (oedema), diffusion-weighted (abscess vs tumour), MR spectroscopy (choline peak in tumour).
- **Why:** Better resolution, can differentiate tumour from oedema, assess for leptomeningeal spread.
- **Key patterns:**  
  - **Glioblastoma:** Irregular ring enhancement, central necrosis, “butterfly” across corpus callosum  
  - **Meningioma:** Extra-axial, dural tail, homogenous enhancement, hyperostosis  
  - **Metastasis:** Multiple, small round, at grey-white junction, uniform ring enhancement  
  - **Abscess:** Diffusion restriction, smooth wall, ring enhancement – **key mimic**

**Histology (via biopsy or resection):**
- **What it shows:** Tumour type, grade, molecular markers (IDH1 mutation, MGMT methylation, 1p/19q codeletion).
- **Why:** Prognosis and treatment decisions (e.g., IDH-mutant gliomas better prognosis; MGMT-methylated glioblastoma more chemo-sensitive).

**Single finding that confirms:**  
- **Biopsy** – a definite diagnosis of malignancy.  
- **Imaging** – multiple ring-enhancing lesions at grey-white junction in a patient with known cancer = almost certainly metastatic.

**Rule-out:**  
- **Normal MRI with gadolinium** (high-sensitivity) makes brain tumour very unlikely – consider seizure workup, metabolic, or functional disorders.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour) – the “raise ICP” protocol:**
- **Call senior / neurosurgery** – any new mass with midline shift >5mm or impending herniation requires immediate neurosurgical opinion.
- **Elevate head of bed to 30°** – improves venous drainage, reduces ICP.
- **Dexamethasone IV 10 mg stat** – then 4-6 mg QDS (oral/IV) – reduces vasogenic oedema (tightens BBB). Delay if isolated mass – only give after imaging confirms no haemorrhage or infection risk (abscess can worsen with steroids).
- **Mannitol 20% 1-2 g/kg IV** if signs of herniation (Cushing’s triad: bradycardia, hypertension, irregular breathing) – temporary measure until theatre.
- **Antiepileptic if seizure** – Levetiracetam 1000 mg IV (loading) – preferred due to low drug interactions (enzyme-inducing AEDs like phenytoin can interfere with steroids/chemo).
- **Do NOT** give prophylactic anticonvulsants for newly discovered tumour without seizure – evidence shows no benefit and side effects.

**Ward (24-72h) – stabilisation and planning:**
- **Continue dexamethasone** – taper as symptoms improve (e.g., reduce by 2 mg every 3-5 days over weeks). Monitor blood glucose, GI prophylaxis (PPI).
- **CT scan or MRI with CT angiogram** if surgical plan – evaluate vascular anatomy.
- **Neurosurgery consult** – decide on biopsy vs maximal safe resection. For a single accessible lesion, resection is preferred for both diagnosis and debulking.
- **If multiple metastases** – whole brain radiotherapy (WBRT) or stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) may be planned. Start dexamethasone before WBRT.
- **Check for primary** if metastatic suspected – CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, PET-CT, tumour markers.

**Long-term – definitive treatment (example regimens):**
- **Glioblastoma (standard)** : Maximal safe resection → concurrent radiotherapy (60 Gy in 30 fractions) + **Temozolomide** 75 mg/m² PO daily during RT → then 6 cycles of adjuvant Temozolomide 150-200 mg/m² PO days 1-5 every 28 days. MGMT methylation predicts response.
- **Low-grade glioma** : Observation vs surgery alone, then radiation +/- chemo (PCV or Temozolomide) for high-risk features (age >40, subtotal resection, IDH wild-type).
- **Meningioma** : If incidental + small → watch. If symptomatic or growing → surgical resection (Simpson grade I). Radiotherapy for subtotal or high-grade/atypical.
- **Metastases (solitary)** : Resection + SRS for single; WBRT if multiple or leptomeningeal. For radiosensitive histologies (e.g., SCLC, germ cell) – consider WBRT first.

**What NOT to do:**
- **Do not start dexamethasone before imaging** if infection possible (abscess) – steroids can mask fever and reduce enhancement, delaying culture.
- **Do not give whole brain RT for a single metastasis if good performance status** – better to resect + SRS to preserve cognition.
- **Do not use prophylactic anticonvulsants** – unnecessary and causes side effects.
- **Do not taper steroids too fast** – rebound oedema, check symptoms.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Brain Tumour (Primary or Metastatic) | Brain Abscess | Cerebral Ischaemia (Stroke) | Multiple Sclerosis (MS) |
|---------|--------------------------------------|--------------|----------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Pain** | Headache (morning, worse with Valsalva, progressive) | Headache, fever, signs of infection | Usually no headache (unless haemorrhagic) | Headache not typical (can have optic neuritis pain) |
| **Exam** | Focal deficit, papilloedema, seizures, cognitive change | May have same, plus meningism (neck stiffness, Kernig’s) | Acute onset of maximal deficit within minutes-hours (stroke) | Relapsing-remitting episodes; optic neuritis, Lhermitte’s sign, Uthhoff phenomenon |
| **Imaging** | MRI: mass, enhancement, oedema. Multiple lesions (met) | Ring-enhancing lesion with central diffusion restriction; smooth wall | DWI restriction (acute ischaemia), no mass effect (unless large) | Periventricular, juxtacortical, Dawsons fingers, enhancing lesions (active plaques) |
| **Labs** | Normal or mild CRP (if no infection). Tumour markers maybe | Raised CRP/PCT, positive blood culture, procalcitonin high | Normal; lipid profile for risk | CSF: oligoclonal bands, elevated IgG index, mild pleocytosis |
| **Key differ** | Histology (biopsy) is definitive | Clinical + diffusion-weighted MRI restriction | Rapid onset + vascular territory; CT/CTA shows occlusion | Age <50, relapsing history, CSF findings, no mass effect |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Major complication 1 – Transtentorial herniation (uncal):**
- **Presentation:** Rapid decline in GCS, ipsilateral fixed/dilated pupil (CN III compression), contralateral hemiparesis (or ipsilateral if Kernohan’s notch), Cushing’s triad, posturing.
- **What to do:** Immediate mannitol, hyperventilation (PaCO2 30-35 mmHg), raise head, call neurosurgery – emergency craniectomy/evacuation.
- **Pattern:** Late exam stem – missed slow-growing tumour then sudden collapse after head CT shows severe midline shift.

**Major complication 2 – Seizure status epilepticus:**
- **Presentation:** Continuous convulsions or non-convulsive status (altered mental state but no tonic-clonic movements) – needs EEG if suspicious.
- **What to do:** ABC, midazolam 10 mg buccal/IM, then levetiracetam 60 mg/kg IV, if refractory, propofol infusion + ITU.
- **Key sign:** New “todd’s paresis” (post-ictal weakness) lasting >24h may indicate underlying tumour.

**Major complication 3 – Hydrocephalus:**
- **Presentation:** Worsening headache, vomiting, decreasing GCS, upgaze palsy (Parinaud), “setting sun” sign in children.
- **Imaging:** Ventriculomegaly with periventricular ooze on MRI.
- **Management:** External ventricular drain (EVD) or VP shunt – urgent neurosurgery.

**Late presentation patterns:**  
A patient with untreated low-grade glioma may present years later with catastrophic bleed, or with secondary glioblastoma after dedifferentiation. Untreated metastasis can cause drop attacks, spinal seeding, or leptomeningeal carcinomatosis.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonic for “Brain tumour red flags on history” – “SUNBURN”:**
- **S** – Seizure (new onset in adult)
- **U** – Unexplained vomiting (morning)
- **N** – New focal neurological deficit (motor/sensory/visual)
- **B** – Behavioural change (frontal lobe)
- **U** – Unremitting headache (progressive, worse with coughing/lying)
- **R** – Recent cancer diagnosis (especially lung, breast, melanoma)
- **N** – Nausea + headache without fever

**Mnemonic for “Causes of ring-enhancing brain lesions” – “MAGIC DR”:**
- **M** – Metastasis
- **A** – Abscess
- **G** – Glioblastoma
- **I** – Infarct (subacute)
- **C** – Contusion
- **D** – Demyelinating disease (tumefactive MS)
- **R** – Radiation necrosis

**High-yield clinical pearls:**
1. **Morning headache + papilloedema = raised ICP until proven otherwise.** Don’t waste time with migraine diagnosis – get imaging.
2. **New-onset seizure in an adult is a brain tumour until proven otherwise.** (Except in known epilepsy or clear provoked cause). The most common cause is metastatic lung cancer.
3. **Multiple ring-enhancing lesions = metastatic disease > abscess > lymphoma.** In an immunocompromised patient, consider toxoplasmosis (multiple ring-enhancing, but usually basal ganglia).
4. **Dexamethasone does NOT treat the tumour** – it only reduces oedema. It can mask progression – always document GCS and neurology before starting.
5. **A “butterfly” across the corpus callosum is classic for glioblastoma** – it spreads along white matter tracts and is unresectable.
6. **Meningiomas are often incidental and found in women over 50.** They are benign but can cause seizures or mass effect if large – watch and scan annually.
7. **In a patient with known cancer and new headache + focal sign, do immediate MRI brain with contrast.** CT can miss small metastases and posterior fossa lesions.

**Common mistakes to avoid:**
- Missing fundoscopy in headache patients.
- Starting antiepileptic drugs without a clear seizure diagnosis (can delay tumour workup).
- Assuming single brain lesion is always a primary – must rule out metastasis with CT chest/abdomen.
- Forgetting to think of brain abscess in febrile patients with ring lesion (especially if dental infection, sinusitis, or IV drug use).

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior (reg/consult) urgently:**
- GCS drop ≥2 points from baseline
- New focal deficit or worsening papilloedema
- Seizure (first, prolonged, or recurrent)
- Suspected herniation (pupil asymmetry, Cushing’s, posturing)
- New hydrocephalus on imaging (ventricular dilation)

**When to call ICU / crash team:**
- GCS ≤8 (protection of airway)
- Status epilepticus (persistent >5 min or recurrence without recovery)
- Signs of herniation requiring immediate ICP management (mannitol, hyperventilation, consider intubation)
- Refractory raised ICP not responding to medical therapy

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS2 ≥7** or single parameter of concern (e.g., RR <8 or >25, SpO2 <91%, systolic BP <90 or >220)
- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** (if septic or haemorrhagic)
- **GCS <9** (consider intubation)
- **Midline shift >10mm** on CT (high risk of herniation – discuss with neurosurgery emergently)
- **CT showing intraventricular haemorrhage or posterior fossa mass** – these can cause rapid deterioration due to hydrocephalus or brainstem compression

---

**Final clinical take-home:**  
When a patient with a new neurological symptom walks in, **think brain tumour first**, do a proper neurological exam including fundoscopy, and get imaging early. Dexamethasone is your friend, but only after you have the scan. Don’t be the doctor who says “it’s just a migraine” and misses a glioblastoma.
